1.play: KING RICHARD II: Old John of Gaunt, time-honour'd Lancaster,

2.play: Hast thou, according to thy oath and band,

3.play: Brought hither Henry Hereford thy bold son,

4.play: Here to make good the boisterous late appeal,

5.play: Which then our leisure would not let us hear,

6.play: Against the Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Mowbray?

7.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: I have, my liege.

8.play: KING RICHARD II: Tell me, moreover, hast thou sounded him,

9.play: If he appeal the duke on ancient malice;

10.play: Or worthily, as a good subject should,

11.play: On some known ground of treachery in him?

12.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: As near as I could sift him on that argument,

13.play: On some apparent danger seen in him

14.play: Aim'd at your highness, no inveterate malice.

15.play: KING RICHARD II: Then call them to our presence; face to face,

16.play: And frowning brow to brow, ourselves will hear

17.play: The accuser and the accused freely speak:

18.play: High-stomach'd are they both, and full of ire,

19.play: In rage deaf as the sea, hasty as fire.

20.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Many years of happy days befal

21.play: My gracious sovereign, my most loving liege!

22.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: Each day still better other's happiness;

23.play: Until the heavens, envying earth's good hap,

24.play: Add an immortal title to your crown!

25.play: KING RICHARD II: We thank you both: yet one but flatters us,

26.play: As well appeareth by the cause you come;

27.play: Namely to appeal each other of high treason.

28.play: Cousin of Hereford, what dost thou object

29.play: Against the Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Mowbray?

30.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: First, heaven be the record to my speech!

31.play: In the devotion of a subject's love,

32.play: Tendering the precious safety of my prince,

33.play: And free from other misbegotten hate,

34.play: Come I appellant to this princely presence.

35.play: Now, Thomas Mowbray, do I turn to thee,

36.play: And mark my greeting well; for what I speak

37.play: My body shall make good upon this earth,

38.play: Or my divine soul answer it in heaven.

39.play: Thou art a traitor and a miscreant,

40.play: Too good to be so and too bad to live,

41.play: Since the more fair and crystal is the sky,

42.play: The uglier seem the clouds that in it fly.

43.play: Once more, the more to aggravate the note,

44.play: With a foul traitor's name stuff I thy throat;

45.play: And wish, so please my sovereign, ere I move,

46.play: What my tongue speaks my right drawn sword may prove.

47.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: Let not my cold words here accuse my zeal:

48.play: 'Tis not the trial of a woman's war,

49.play: The bitter clamour of two eager tongues,

50.play: Can arbitrate this cause betwixt us twain;

51.play: The blood is hot that must be cool'd for this:

52.play: Yet can I not of such tame patience boast

53.play: As to be hush'd and nought at all to say:

54.play: First, the fair reverence of your highness curbs me

55.play: From giving reins and spurs to my free speech;

56.play: Which else would post until it had return'd

57.play: These terms of treason doubled down his throat.

58.play: Setting aside his high blood's royalty,

59.play: And let him be no kinsman to my liege,

60.play: I do defy him, and I spit at him;

61.play: Call him a slanderous coward and a villain:

62.play: Which to maintain I would allow him odds,

63.play: And meet him, were I tied to run afoot

64.play: Even to the frozen ridges of the Alps,

66.play: Where ever Englishman durst set his foot.

67.play: Mean time let this defend my loyalty,

68.play: By all my hopes, most falsely doth he lie.

69.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Pale trembling coward, there I throw my gage,

70.play: Disclaiming here the kindred of the king,

71.play: And lay aside my high blood's royalty,

72.play: Which fear, not reverence, makes thee to except.

73.play: If guilty dread have left thee so much strength

74.play: As to take up mine honour's pawn, then stoop:

75.play: By that and all the rites of knighthood else,

76.play: Will I make good against thee, arm to arm,

77.play: What I have spoke, or thou canst worse devise.

78.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: I take it up; and by that sword I swear

79.play: Which gently laid my knighthood on my shoulder,

80.play: I'll answer thee in any fair degree,

81.play: Or chivalrous design of knightly trial:

82.play: And when I mount, alive may I not light,

83.play: If I be traitor or unjustly fight!

84.play: KING RICHARD II: What doth our cousin lay to Mowbray's charge?

85.play: It must be great that can inherit us

86.play: So much as of a thought of ill in him.

87.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Look, what I speak, my life shall prove it true;

88.play: That Mowbray hath received eight thousand nobles

89.play: In name of lendings for your highness' soldiers,

90.play: The which he hath detain'd for lewd employments,

91.play: Like a false traitor and injurious villain.

92.play: Besides I say and will in battle prove,

93.play: Or here or elsewhere to the furthest verge

94.play: That ever was survey'd by English eye,

95.play: That all the treasons for these eighteen years

96.play: Complotted and contrived in this land

97.play: Fetch from false Mowbray their first head and spring.

98.play: Further I say and further will maintain

99.play: Upon his bad life to make all this good,

100.play: That he did plot the Duke of Gloucester's death,

102.play: And consequently, like a traitor coward,

103.play: Sluiced out his innocent soul through streams of blood:

104.play: Which blood, like sacrificing Abel's, cries,

105.play: Even from the tongueless caverns of the earth,

106.play: To me for justice and rough chastisement;

107.play: And, by the glorious worth of my descent,

108.play: This arm shall do it, or this life be spent.

109.play: KING RICHARD II: How high a pitch his resolution soars!

110.play: Thomas of Norfolk, what say'st thou to this?

111.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: O, let my sovereign turn away his face

112.play: And bid his ears a little while be deaf,

113.play: Till I have told this slander of his blood,

114.play: How God and good men hate so foul a liar.

115.play: KING RICHARD II: Mowbray, impartial are our eyes and ears:

116.play: Were he my brother, nay, my kingdom's heir,

117.play: As he is but my father's brother's son,

118.play: Now, by my sceptre's awe, I make a vow,

119.play: Such neighbour nearness to our sacred blood

120.play: Should nothing privilege him, nor partialize

121.play: The unstooping firmness of my upright soul:

122.play: He is our subject, Mowbray; so art thou:

123.play: Free speech and fearless I to thee allow.

124.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: Then, Bolingbroke, as low as to thy heart,

125.play: Through the false passage of thy throat, thou liest.

126.play: Three parts of that receipt I had for Calais

127.play: Disbursed I duly to his highness' soldiers;

128.play: The other part reserved I by consent,

129.play: For that my sovereign liege was in my debt

130.play: Upon remainder of a dear account,

131.play: Since last I went to France to fetch his queen:

132.play: Now swallow down that lie. For Gloucester's death,

133.play: I slew him not; but to my own disgrace

134.play: Neglected my sworn duty in that case.

135.play: For you, my noble Lord of Lancaster,

136.play: The honourable father to my foe

137.play: Once did I lay an ambush for your life,

138.play: A trespass that doth vex my grieved soul

139.play: But ere I last received the sacrament

140.play: I did confess it, and exactly begg'd

141.play: Your grace's pardon, and I hope I had it.

142.play: This is my fault: as for the rest appeall'd,

143.play: It issues from the rancour of a villain,

144.play: A recreant and most degenerate traitor

145.play: Which in myself I boldly will defend;

146.play: And interchangeably hurl down my gage

148.play: To prove myself a loyal gentleman

149.play: Even in the best blood chamber'd in his bosom.

150.play: In haste whereof, most heartily I pray

151.play: Your highness to assign our trial day.

152.play: KING RICHARD II: Wrath-kindled gentlemen, be ruled by me;

153.play: Let's purge this choler without letting blood:

154.play: This we prescribe, though no physician;

155.play: Deep malice makes too deep incision;

156.play: Forget, forgive; conclude and be agreed;

157.play: Our doctors say this is no month to bleed.

158.play: Good uncle, let this end where it begun;

159.play: We'll calm the Duke of Norfolk, you your son.

160.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: To be a make-peace shall become my age:

161.play: Throw down, my son, the Duke of Norfolk's gage.

162.play: KING RICHARD II: And, Norfolk, throw down his.

163.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: When, Harry, when?

164.play: Obedience bids I should not bid again.

165.play: KING RICHARD II: Norfolk, throw down, we bid; there is no boot.

166.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: Myself I throw, dread sovereign, at thy foot.

167.play: My life thou shalt command, but not my shame:

168.play: The one my duty owes; but my fair name,

169.play: Despite of death that lives upon my grave,

170.play: To dark dishonour's use thou shalt not have.

171.play: I am disgraced, impeach'd and baffled here,

172.play: Pierced to the soul with slander's venom'd spear,

173.play: The which no balm can cure but his heart-blood

175.play: KING RICHARD II: Rage must be withstood:

176.play: Give me his gage: lions make leopards tame.

177.play: THOMAS MOWBRAY: Yea, but not change his spots: take but my shame.

178.play: And I resign my gage. My dear dear lord,

179.play: The purest treasure mortal times afford

181.play: Men are but gilded loam or painted clay.

182.play: A jewel in a ten-times-barr'd-up chest

183.play: Is a bold spirit in a loyal breast.

184.play: Mine honour is my life; both grow in one:

185.play: Take honour from me, and my life is done:

186.play: Then, dear my liege, mine honour let me try;

187.play: In that I live and for that will I die.

188.play: KING RICHARD II: Cousin, throw up your gage; do you begin.

189.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: O, God defend my soul from such deep sin!

190.play: Shall I seem crest-fall'n in my father's sight?

191.play: Or with pale beggar-fear impeach my height

192.play: Before this out-dared dastard? Ere my tongue

193.play: Shall wound my honour with such feeble wrong,

194.play: Or sound so base a parle, my teeth shall tear

195.play: The slavish motive of recanting fear,

196.play: And spit it bleeding in his high disgrace,

197.play: Where shame doth harbour, even in Mowbray's face.

198.play: KING RICHARD II: We were not born to sue, but to command;

199.play: Which since we cannot do to make you friends,

200.play: Be ready, as your lives shall answer it,

201.play: At Coventry, upon Saint Lambert's day:

202.play: There shall your swords and lances arbitrate

203.play: The swelling difference of your settled hate:

204.play: Since we can not atone you, we shall see

206.play: Lord marshal, command our officers at arms

207.play: Be ready to direct these home alarms.

1.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: Alas, the part I had in Woodstock's blood

2.play: Doth more solicit me than your exclaims,

3.play: To stir against the butchers of his life!

4.play: But since correction lieth in those hands

5.play: Which made the fault that we cannot correct,

6.play: Put we our quarrel to the will of heaven;

7.play: Who, when they see the hours ripe on earth,

8.play: Will rain hot vengeance on offenders' heads.

9.play: DUCHESS: Finds brotherhood in thee no sharper spur?

10.play: Hath love in thy old blood no living fire?

11.play: Edward's seven sons, whereof thyself art one,

12.play: Were as seven vials of his sacred blood,

13.play: Or seven fair branches springing from one root:

14.play: Some of those seven are dried by nature's course,

15.play: Some of those branches by the Destinies cut;

16.play: But Thomas, my dear lord, my life, my Gloucester,

17.play: One vial full of Edward's sacred blood,

18.play: One flourishing branch of his most royal root,

19.play: Is crack'd, and all the precious liquor spilt,

20.play: Is hack'd down, and his summer leaves all faded,

21.play: By envy's hand and murder's bloody axe.

22.play: Ah, Gaunt, his blood was thine! that bed, that womb,

23.play: That metal, that self-mould, that fashion'd thee

24.play: Made him a man; and though thou livest and breathest,

25.play: Yet art thou slain in him: thou dost consent

26.play: In some large measure to thy father's death,

27.play: In that thou seest thy wretched brother die,

28.play: Who was the model of thy father's life.

29.play: Call it not patience, Gaunt; it is despair:

30.play: In suffering thus thy brother to be slaughter'd,

31.play: Thou showest the naked pathway to thy life,

32.play: Teaching stern murder how to butcher thee:

33.play: That which in mean men we intitle patience

34.play: Is pale cold cowardice in noble breasts.

35.play: What shall I say? to safeguard thine own life,

36.play: The best way is to venge my Gloucester's death.

37.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: God's is the quarrel; for God's substitute,

38.play: His deputy anointed in His sight,

39.play: Hath caused his death: the which if wrongfully,

40.play: Let heaven revenge; for I may never lift

41.play: An angry arm against His minister.

42.play: Where then, alas, may I complain myself?

43.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: To God, the widow's champion and defence.

44.play: Why, then, I will. Farewell, old Gaunt.

45.play: Thou goest to Coventry, there to behold

46.play: Our cousin Hereford and fell Mowbray fight:

47.play: O, sit my husband's wrongs on Hereford's spear,

48.play: That it may enter butcher Mowbray's breast!

49.play: Or, if misfortune miss the first career,

50.play: Be Mowbray's sins so heavy in his bosom,

51.play: They may break his foaming courser's back,

52.play: And throw the rider headlong in the lists,

53.play: A caitiff recreant to my cousin Hereford!

54.play: Farewell, old Gaunt: thy sometimes brother's wife

55.play: With her companion grief must end her life.

56.play: JOHN OF GAUNT: Sister, farewell; I must to Coventry:

57.play: As much good stay with thee as go with me!

58.play: Yet one word more: grief boundeth where it falls,

59.play: Not with the empty hollowness, but weight:

60.play: I take my leave before I have begun,

61.play: For sorrow ends not when it seemeth done.

62.play: Commend me to thy brother, Edmund York.

63.play: Lo, this is all: --nay, yet depart not so;

64.play: Though this be all, do not so quickly go;

65.play: I shall remember more. Bid him--ah, what?--

66.play: With all good speed at Plashy visit me.

67.play: Alack, and what shall good old York there see

68.play: But empty lodgings and unfurnish'd walls,

70.play: And what hear there for welcome but my groans?

71.play: Therefore commend me; let him not come there,

72.play: To seek out sorrow that dwells every where.

73.play: Desolate, desolate, will I hence and die:

74.play: The last leave of thee takes my weeping eye.

213.play: Return with welcome home from banishment.

55.play: Suddenly taken; and hath sent post haste

57.play: KING RICHARD II: Where lies he?

299.play: Stay and be secret, and myself will go.

300.play: LORD ROSS: To horse, to horse! urge doubts to them that fear.

301.play: LORD WILLOUGHBY: Hold out my horse, and I will first be there.

1.play: BUSHY: Madam, your majesty is too much sad:

2.play: You promised, when you parted with the king,

5.play: QUEEN: To please the king I did; to please myself

6.play: I cannot do it; yet I know no cause

7.play: Why I should welcome such a guest as grief,

8.play: Save bidding farewell to so sweet a guest

9.play: As my sweet Richard: yet again, methinks,

10.play: Some unborn sorrow, ripe in fortune's womb,

11.play: Is coming towards me, and my inward soul

12.play: With nothing trembles: at some thing it grieves,

13.play: More than with parting from my lord the king.

14.play: BUSHY: Each substance of a grief hath twenty shadows,

15.play: Which shows like grief itself, but is not so;

16.play: For sorrow's eye, glazed with blinding tears,

17.play: Divides one thing entire to many objects;

18.play: Like perspectives, which rightly gazed upon

19.play: Show nothing but confusion, eyed awry

20.play: Distinguish form: so your sweet majesty,

21.play: Looking awry upon your lord's departure,

22.play: Find shapes of grief, more than himself, to wail;

23.play: Which, look'd on as it is, is nought but shadows

24.play: Of what it is not. Then, thrice-gracious queen,

25.play: More than your lord's departure weep not: more's not seen;

26.play: Or if it be, 'tis with false sorrow's eye,

27.play: Which for things true weeps things imaginary.

28.play: QUEEN: It may be so; but yet my inward soul

29.play: Persuades me it is otherwise: howe'er it be,

30.play: I cannot but be sad; so heavy sad

31.play: As, though on thinking on no thought I think,

32.play: Makes me with heavy nothing faint and shrink.

33.play: BUSHY: 'Tis nothing but conceit, my gracious lady.

34.play: QUEEN: 'Tis nothing less: conceit is still derived

35.play: From some forefather grief; mine is not so,

36.play: For nothing had begot my something grief;

37.play: Or something hath the nothing that I grieve:

38.play: 'Tis in reversion that I do possess;

39.play: But what it is, that is not yet known; what

40.play: I cannot name; 'tis nameless woe, I wot.

41.play: GREEN: God save your majesty! and well met, gentlemen:

42.play: I hope the king is not yet shipp'd for Ireland.

43.play: QUEEN: Why hopest thou so? 'tis better hope he is;

44.play: For his designs crave haste, his haste good hope:

45.play: Then wherefore dost thou hope he is not shipp'd?

46.play: GREEN: That he, our hope, might have retired his power,

47.play: And driven into despair an enemy's hope,

48.play: Who strongly hath set footing in this land:

50.play: And with uplifted arms is safe arrived

52.play: QUEEN: Now God in heaven forbid!

53.play: GREEN: Ah, madam, 'tis too true: and that is worse,

54.play: The Lord Northumberland, his son young Henry Percy,

55.play: The Lords of Ross, Beaumond, and Willoughby,

56.play: With all their powerful friends, are fled to him.

57.play: BUSHY: Why have you not proclaim'd Northumberland

58.play: And all the rest revolted faction traitors?

59.play: GREEN: We have: whereupon the Earl of Worcester

60.play: Hath broke his staff, resign'd his stewardship,

61.play: And all the household servants fled with him

63.play: QUEEN: So, Green, thou art the midwife to my woe,

64.play: And Bolingbroke my sorrow's dismal heir:

65.play: Now hath my soul brought forth her prodigy,

66.play: And I, a gasping new-deliver'd mother,

67.play: Have woe to woe, sorrow to sorrow join'd.

70.play: I will despair, and be at enmity

71.play: With cozening hope: he is a flatterer,

72.play: A parasite, a keeper back of death,

73.play: Who gently would dissolve the bands of life,

74.play: Which false hope lingers in extremity.

75.play: GREEN: Here comes the Duke of York.

76.play: QUEEN: With signs of war about his aged neck:

77.play: O, full of careful business are his looks!

78.play: Uncle, for God's sake, speak comfortable words.

79.play: DUKE OF YORK: Should I do so, I should belie my thoughts:

80.play: Comfort's in heaven; and we are on the earth,

81.play: Where nothing lives but crosses, cares and grief.

82.play: Your husband, he is gone to save far off,

83.play: Whilst others come to make him lose at home:

84.play: Here am I left to underprop his land,

85.play: Who, weak with age, cannot support myself:

86.play: Now comes the sick hour that his surfeit made;

87.play: Now shall he try his friends that flatter'd him.

88.play: Servant: My lord, your son was gone before I came.

89.play: He was? Why, so! go all which way it will!

90.play: The nobles they are fled, the commons they are cold,

91.play: And will, I fear, revolt on Hereford's side.

92.play: Sirrah, get thee to Plashy, to my sister Gloucester;

93.play: Bid her send me presently a thousand pound:

95.play: My lord, I had forgot to tell your lordship,

96.play: To-day, as I came by, I called there;

97.play: But I shall grieve you to report the rest.

99.play: An hour before I came, the duchess died.

100.play: God for his mercy! what a tide of woes

101.play: Comes rushing on this woeful land at once!

102.play: I know not what to do: I would to God,

103.play: So my untruth had not provoked him to it,

104.play: The king had cut off my head with my brother's.

105.play: What, are there no posts dispatch'd for Ireland?

106.play: How shall we do for money for these wars?

107.play: Come, sister,--cousin, I would say--pray, pardon me.

108.play: Go, fellow, get thee home, provide some carts

109.play: And bring away the armour that is there.

110.play: Gentlemen, will you go muster men?

111.play: If I know how or which way to order these affairs

112.play: Thus thrust disorderly into my hands,

113.play: Never believe me. Both are my kinsmen:

114.play: The one is my sovereign, whom both my oath

115.play: And duty bids defend; the other again

116.play: Is my kinsman, whom the king hath wrong'd,

117.play: Whom conscience and my kindred bids to right.

118.play: Well, somewhat we must do. Come, cousin, I'll

120.play: Gentlemen, go, muster up your men,

121.play: And meet me presently at Berkeley.

123.play: But time will not permit: all is uneven,

124.play: And every thing is left at six and seven.

125.play: BUSHY: The wind sits fair for news to go to Ireland,

126.play: But none returns. For us to levy power

129.play: GREEN: Besides, our nearness to the king in love

130.play: Is near the hate of those love not the king.

131.play: BAGOT: And that's the wavering commons: for their love

132.play: Lies in their purses, and whoso empties them

133.play: By so much fills their hearts with deadly hate.

134.play: BUSHY: Wherein the king stands generally condemn'd.

135.play: BAGOT: If judgement lie in them, then so do we,

136.play: Because we ever have been near the king.

137.play: GREEN: Well, I will for refuge straight to Bristol castle:

138.play: The Earl of Wiltshire is already there.

139.play: BUSHY: Thither will I with you; for little office

140.play: The hateful commons will perform for us,

141.play: Except like curs to tear us all to pieces.

142.play: Will you go along with us?

143.play: BAGOT: No; I will to Ireland to his majesty.

144.play: Farewell: if heart's presages be not vain,

145.play: We three here art that ne'er shall meet again.

146.play: BUSHY: That's as York thrives to beat back Bolingbroke.

147.play: GREEN: Alas, poor duke! the task he undertakes

148.play: Is numbering sands and drinking oceans dry:

149.play: Where one on his side fights, thousands will fly.

150.play: Farewell at once, for once, for all, and ever.

151.play: BUSHY: Well, we may meet again.

1.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: How far is it, my lord, to Berkeley now?

3.play: I am a stranger here in Gloucestershire:

4.play: These high wild hills and rough uneven ways

5.play: Draws out our miles, and makes them wearisome,

6.play: And yet your fair discourse hath been as sugar,

7.play: Making the hard way sweet and delectable.

8.play: But I bethink me what a weary way

9.play: From Ravenspurgh to Cotswold will be found

10.play: In Ross and Willoughby, wanting your company,

11.play: Which, I protest, hath very much beguiled

12.play: The tediousness and process of my travel:

13.play: But theirs is sweetened with the hope to have

14.play: The present benefit which I possess;

15.play: And hope to joy is little less in joy

16.play: Than hope enjoy'd: by this the weary lords

17.play: Shall make their way seem short, as mine hath done

18.play: By sight of what I have, your noble company.

19.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Of much less value is my company

20.play: Than your good words. But who comes here?

21.play: It is my son, young Harry Percy,

22.play: Sent from my brother Worcester, whencesoever.

24.play: HENRY PERCY: I had thought, my lord, to have learn'd his health of you.

25.play: Why, is he not with the queen?

26.play: HENRY PERCY: No, my good Lord; he hath forsook the court,

27.play: Broken his staff of office and dispersed

30.play: He was not so resolved when last we spake together.

31.play: HENRY PERCY: Because your lordship was proclaimed traitor.

32.play: But he, my lord, is gone to Ravenspurgh,

33.play: To offer service to the Duke of Hereford,

34.play: And sent me over by Berkeley, to discover

35.play: What power the Duke of York had levied there;

36.play: Then with directions to repair to Ravenspurgh.

37.play: Have you forgot the Duke of Hereford, boy?

38.play: HENRY PERCY: No, my good lord, for that is not forgot

39.play: Which ne'er I did remember: to my knowledge,

40.play: I never in my life did look on him.

41.play: Then learn to know him now; this is the duke.

42.play: HENRY PERCY: My gracious lord, I tender you my service,

43.play: Such as it is, being tender, raw and young:

44.play: Which elder days shall ripen and confirm

45.play: To more approved service and desert.

46.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: I thank thee, gentle Percy; and be sure

47.play: I count myself in nothing else so happy

48.play: As in a soul remembering my good friends;

49.play: And, as my fortune ripens with thy love,

50.play: It shall be still thy true love's recompense:

51.play: My heart this covenant makes, my hand thus seals it.

52.play: How far is it to Berkeley? and what stir

53.play: Keeps good old York there with his men of war?

54.play: HENRY PERCY: There stands the castle, by yon tuft of trees,

55.play: Mann'd with three hundred men, as I have heard;

56.play: And in it are the Lords of York, Berkeley, and Seymour;

57.play: None else of name and noble estimate.

58.play: Here come the Lords of Ross and Willoughby,

59.play: Bloody with spurring, fiery-red with haste.

60.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Welcome, my lords. I wot your love pursues

61.play: A banish'd traitor: all my treasury

62.play: Is yet but unfelt thanks, which more enrich'd

63.play: Shall be your love and labour's recompense.

64.play: LORD ROSS: Your presence makes us rich, most noble lord.

65.play: LORD WILLOUGHBY: And far surmounts our labour to attain it.

66.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Evermore thanks, the exchequer of the poor;

67.play: Which, till my infant fortune comes to years,

68.play: Stands for my bounty. But who comes here?

69.play: It is my Lord of Berkeley, as I guess.

70.play: LORD BERKELEY: My Lord of Hereford, my message is to you.

71.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: My lord, my answer is--to Lancaster;

72.play: And I am come to seek that name in England;

73.play: And I must find that title in your tongue,

74.play: Before I make reply to aught you say.

75.play: LORD BERKELEY: Mistake me not, my lord; 'tis not my meaning

76.play: To raze one title of your honour out:

77.play: To you, my lord, I come, what lord you will,

78.play: From the most gracious regent of this land,

79.play: The Duke of York, to know what pricks you on

80.play: To take advantage of the absent time

81.play: And fright our native peace with self-born arms.

82.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: I shall not need transport my words by you;

83.play: Here comes his grace in person. My noble uncle!

84.play: DUKE OF YORK: Show me thy humble heart, and not thy knee,

85.play: Whose duty is deceiveable and false.

88.play: Grace me no grace, nor uncle me no uncle:

89.play: I am no traitor's uncle; and that word 'grace.'

90.play: In an ungracious mouth is but profane.

91.play: Why have those banish'd and forbidden legs

92.play: Dared once to touch a dust of England's ground?

93.play: But then more 'why?' why have they dared to march

94.play: So many miles upon her peaceful bosom,

95.play: Frighting her pale-faced villages with war

97.play: Comest thou because the anointed king is hence?

98.play: Why, foolish boy, the king is left behind,

99.play: And in my loyal bosom lies his power.

100.play: Were I but now the lord of such hot youth

101.play: As when brave Gaunt, thy father, and myself

102.play: Rescued the Black Prince, that young Mars of men,

103.play: From forth the ranks of many thousand French,

104.play: O, then how quickly should this arm of mine.

105.play: Now prisoner to the palsy, chastise thee

106.play: And minister correction to thy fault!

107.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: My gracious uncle, let me know my fault:

108.play: On what condition stands it and wherein?

109.play: DUKE OF YORK: Even in condition of the worst degree,

110.play: In gross rebellion and detested treason:

111.play: Thou art a banish'd man, and here art come

112.play: Before the expiration of thy time,

113.play: In braving arms against thy sovereign.

114.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: As I was banish'd, I was banish'd Hereford;

115.play: But as I come, I come for Lancaster.

116.play: And, noble uncle, I beseech your grace

117.play: Look on my wrongs with an indifferent eye:

118.play: You are my father, for methinks in you

119.play: I see old Gaunt alive; O, then, my father,

120.play: Will you permit that I shall stand condemn'd

121.play: A wandering vagabond; my rights and royalties

122.play: Pluck'd from my arms perforce and given away

123.play: To upstart unthrifts? Wherefore was I born?

124.play: If that my cousin king be King of England,

125.play: It must be granted I am Duke of Lancaster.

126.play: You have a son, Aumerle, my noble cousin;

127.play: Had you first died, and he been thus trod down,

128.play: He should have found his uncle Gaunt a father,

129.play: To rouse his wrongs and chase them to the bay.

130.play: I am denied to sue my livery here,

131.play: And yet my letters-patents give me leave:

132.play: My father's goods are all distrain'd and sold,

133.play: And these and all are all amiss employ'd.

134.play: What would you have me do? I am a subject,

135.play: And I challenge law: attorneys are denied me;

136.play: And therefore, personally I lay my claim

137.play: To my inheritance of free descent.

138.play: The noble duke hath been too much abused.

139.play: LORD ROSS: It stands your grace upon to do him right.

140.play: LORD WILLOUGHBY: Base men by his endowments are made great.

141.play: DUKE OF YORK: My lords of England, let me tell you this:

142.play: I have had feeling of my cousin's wrongs

143.play: And laboured all I could to do him right;

144.play: But in this kind to come, in braving arms,

145.play: Be his own carver and cut out his way,

146.play: To find out right with wrong, it may not be;

147.play: And you that do abet him in this kind

148.play: Cherish rebellion and are rebels all.

149.play: The noble duke hath sworn his coming is

150.play: But for his own; and for the right of that

151.play: We all have strongly sworn to give him aid;

152.play: And let him ne'er see joy that breaks that oath!

153.play: DUKE OF YORK: Well, well, I see the issue of these arms:

154.play: I cannot mend it, I must needs confess,

155.play: Because my power is weak and all ill left:

156.play: But if I could, by Him that gave me life,

157.play: I would attach you all and make you stoop

158.play: Unto the sovereign mercy of the king;

159.play: But since I cannot, be it known to you

160.play: I do remain as neuter. So, fare you well;

161.play: Unless you please to enter in the castle

162.play: And there repose you for this night.

163.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: An offer, uncle, that we will accept:

164.play: But we must win your grace to go with us

165.play: To Bristol castle, which they say is held

166.play: By Bushy, Bagot and their complices,

168.play: Which I have sworn to weed and pluck away.

169.play: DUKE OF YORK: It may be I will go with you: but yet I'll pause;

170.play: For I am loath to break our country's laws.

171.play: Nor friends nor foes, to me welcome you are:

172.play: Things past redress are now with me past care.

1.play: Captain: My lord of Salisbury, we have stay'd ten days,

2.play: And hardly kept our countrymen together,

3.play: And yet we hear no tidings from the king;

4.play: Therefore we will disperse ourselves: farewell.

5.play: EARL OF SALISBURY: Stay yet another day, thou trusty Welshman:

6.play: The king reposeth all his confidence in thee.

7.play: 'Tis thought the king is dead; we will not stay.

8.play: The bay-trees in our country are all wither'd

9.play: And meteors fright the fixed stars of heaven;

10.play: The pale-faced moon looks bloody on the earth

11.play: And lean-look'd prophets whisper fearful change;

12.play: Rich men look sad and ruffians dance and leap,

13.play: The one in fear to lose what they enjoy,

14.play: The other to enjoy by rage and war:

15.play: These signs forerun the death or fall of kings.

16.play: Farewell: our countrymen are gone and fled,

17.play: As well assured Richard their king is dead.

1.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Bring forth these men.

2.play: Bushy and Green, I will not vex your souls--

3.play: Since presently your souls must part your bodies--

4.play: With too much urging your pernicious lives,

5.play: For 'twere no charity; yet, to wash your blood

6.play: From off my hands, here in the view of men

7.play: I will unfold some causes of your deaths.

8.play: You have misled a prince, a royal king,

9.play: A happy gentleman in blood and lineaments,

10.play: By you unhappied and disfigured clean:

11.play: You have in manner with your sinful hours

12.play: Made a divorce betwixt his queen and him,

13.play: Broke the possession of a royal bed

14.play: And stain'd the beauty of a fair queen's cheeks

15.play: With tears drawn from her eyes by your foul wrongs.

16.play: Myself, a prince by fortune of my birth,

17.play: Near to the king in blood, and near in love

18.play: Till you did make him misinterpret me,

19.play: Have stoop'd my neck under your injuries,

20.play: And sigh'd my English breath in foreign clouds,

21.play: Eating the bitter bread of banishment;

22.play: Whilst you have fed upon my signories,

23.play: Dispark'd my parks and fell'd my forest woods,

24.play: From my own windows torn my household coat,

25.play: Razed out my imprese, leaving me no sign,

26.play: Save men's opinions and my living blood,

27.play: To show the world I am a gentleman.

28.play: This and much more, much more than twice all this,

29.play: Condemns you to the death. See them deliver'd over

30.play: To execution and the hand of death.

31.play: BUSHY: More welcome is the stroke of death to me

32.play: Than Bolingbroke to England. Lords, farewell.

33.play: GREEN: My comfort is that heaven will take our souls

34.play: And plague injustice with the pains of hell.

35.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: My Lord Northumberland, see them dispatch'd.

36.play: Uncle, you say the queen is at your house;

37.play: For God's sake, fairly let her be entreated:

38.play: Tell her I send to her my kind commends;

39.play: Take special care my greetings be deliver'd.

40.play: DUKE OF YORK: A gentleman of mine I have dispatch'd

41.play: With letters of your love to her at large.

42.play: HENRY BOLINGBROKE: Thank, gentle uncle. Come, lords, away.

43.play: To fight with Glendower and his complices:

44.play: Awhile to work, and after holiday.

1.play: KING RICHARD II: Barkloughly castle call they this at hand?

2.play: DUKE OF AUMERLE: Yea, my lord. How brooks your grace the air,

3.play: After your late tossing on the breaking seas?

4.play: KING RICHARD II: Needs must I like it well: I weep for joy

5.play: To stand upon my kingdom once again.

6.play: Dear earth, I do salute thee with my hand,

7.play: Though rebels wound thee with their horses' hoofs:

8.play: As a long-parted mother with her child

9.play: Plays fondly with her tears and smiles in meeting,

10.play: So, weeping, smiling, greet I thee, my earth,

11.play: And do thee favours with my royal hands.

12.play: Feed not thy sovereign's foe, my gentle earth,

13.play: Nor with thy sweets comfort his ravenous sense;

14.play: But let thy spiders, that suck up thy venom,

15.play: And heavy-gaited toads lie in their way,

16.play: Doing annoyance to the treacherous feet

17.play: Which with usurping steps do trample thee:

18.play: Yield stinging nettles to mine enemies;

19.play: And when they from thy bosom pluck a flower,

20.play: Guard it, I pray thee, with a lurking adder

21.play: Whose double tongue may with a mortal touch

22.play: Throw death upon thy sovereign's enemies.

23.play: Mock not my senseless conjuration, lords:

24.play: This earth shall have a feeling and these stones

25.play: Prove armed soldiers, ere her native king

26.play: Shall falter under foul rebellion's arms.

27.play: BISHOP OF CARLISLE: Fear not, my lord: that Power that made you king

28.play: Hath power to keep you king in spite of all.

29.play: The means that heaven yields must be embraced,

30.play: And not neglected; else, if heaven would,

31.play: And we will not, heaven's offer we refuse,

32.play: The proffer'd means of succor and redress.

33.play: DUKE OF AUMERLE: He means, my lord, that we are too remiss;

35.play: Grows strong and great in substance and in power.

36.play: KING RICHARD II: Discomfortable cousin! know'st thou not

37.play: That when the searching eye of heaven is hid,

38.play: Behind the globe, that lights the lower world,

39.play: Then thieves and robbers range abroad unseen

40.play: In murders and in outrage, boldly here;

41.play: But when from under this terrestrial ball

42.play: He fires the proud tops of the eastern pines

43.play: And darts his light through every guilty hole,

44.play: Then murders, treasons and detested sins,

45.play: The cloak of night being pluck'd from off their backs,

46.play: Stand bare and naked, trembling at themselves?

47.play: So when this thief, this traitor, Bolingbroke,

48.play: Who all this while hath revell'd in the night

49.play: Whilst we were wandering with the antipodes,

50.play: Shall see us rising in our throne, the east,

51.play: His treasons will sit blushing in his face,

52.play: Not able to endure the sight of day,

53.play: But self-affrighted tremble at his sin.

54.play: Not all the water in the rough rude sea

55.play: Can wash the balm off from an anointed king;

56.play: The breath of worldly men cannot depose

57.play: The deputy elected by the Lord:

58.play: For every man that Bolingbroke hath press'd

59.play: To lift shrewd steel against our golden crown,

60.play: God for his Richard hath in heavenly pay

61.play: A glorious angel: then, if angels fight,

62.play: Weak men must fall, for heaven still guards the right.

63.play: Welcome, my lord how far off lies your power?

64.play: EARL OF SALISBURY: Nor near nor farther off, my gracious lord,

65.play: Than this weak arm: discomfort guides my tongue

66.play: And bids me speak of nothing but despair.

67.play: One day too late, I fear me, noble lord,

68.play: Hath clouded all thy happy days on earth:

69.play: O, call back yesterday, bid time return,

70.play: And thou shalt have twelve thousand fighting men!

71.play: To-day, to-day, unhappy day, too late,

72.play: O'erthrows thy joys, friends, fortune and thy state:

73.play: For all the Welshmen, hearing thou wert dead.

74.play: Are gone to Bolingbroke, dispersed and fled.

75.play: DUKE OF AUMERLE: Comfort, my liege; why looks your grace so pale?

76.play: KING RICHARD II: But now the blood of twenty thousand men

77.play: Did triumph in my face, and they are fled;

78.play: And, till so much blood thither come again,

79.play: Have I not reason to look pale and dead?

80.play: All souls that will be safe fly from my side,

81.play: For time hath set a blot upon my pride.

82.play: DUKE OF AUMERLE: Comfort, my liege; remember who you are.

83.play: KING RICHARD II: I had forgot myself; am I not king?

84.play: Awake, thou coward majesty! thou sleepest.

85.play: Is not the king's name twenty thousand names?

86.play: Arm, arm, my name! a puny subject strikes

87.play: At thy great glory. Look not to the ground,

88.play: Ye favourites of a king: are we not high?

89.play: High be our thoughts: I know my uncle York

90.play: Hath power enough to serve our turn. But who comes here?

91.play: SIR STEPHEN SCROOP: More health and happiness betide my liege

92.play: Than can my care-tuned tongue deliver him!

93.play: KING RICHARD II: Mine ear is open and my heart prepared;

94.play: The worst is worldly loss thou canst unfold.

95.play: Say, is my kingdom lost? why, 'twas my care

74.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: [Within] What ho, my liege! for God's sake,

1.play: KING RICHARD II: I have been studying how I may compare

2.play: This prison where I live unto the world:

3.play: And for because the world is populous

4.play: And here is not a creature but myself,

5.play: I cannot do it; yet I'll hammer it out.

6.play: My brain I'll prove the female to my soul,

7.play: My soul the father; and these two beget

9.play: And these same thoughts people this little world,

10.play: In humours like the people of this world,

11.play: For no thought is contented. The better sort,

12.play: As thoughts of things divine, are intermix'd

13.play: With scruples and do set the word itself

15.play: As thus, 'Come, little ones,' and then again,

16.play: 'It is as hard to come as for a camel

17.play: To thread the postern of a small needle's eye.'

18.play: Thoughts tending to ambition, they do plot

19.play: Unlikely wonders; how these vain weak nails

20.play: May tear a passage through the flinty ribs

21.play: Of this hard world, my ragged prison walls,

22.play: And, for they cannot, die in their own pride.

23.play: Thoughts tending to content flatter themselves

24.play: That they are not the first of fortune's slaves,

25.play: Nor shall not be the last; like silly beggars

26.play: Who sitting in the stocks refuge their shame,

27.play: That many have and others must sit there;

28.play: And in this thought they find a kind of ease,

29.play: Bearing their own misfortunes on the back

30.play: Of such as have before endured the like.

31.play: Thus play I in one person many people,

32.play: And none contented: sometimes am I king;

33.play: Then treasons make me wish myself a beggar,

34.play: And so I am: then crushing penury

35.play: Persuades me I was better when a king;

36.play: Then am I king'd again: and by and by

37.play: Think that I am unking'd by Bolingbroke,

38.play: And straight am nothing: but whate'er I be,

39.play: Nor I nor any man that but man is

40.play: With nothing shall be pleased, till he be eased

41.play: With being nothing. Music do I hear?

42.play: Ha, ha! keep time: how sour sweet music is,

43.play: When time is broke and no proportion kept!

44.play: So is it in the music of men's lives.

45.play: And here have I the daintiness of ear

46.play: To cheque time broke in a disorder'd string;

47.play: But for the concord of my state and time

48.play: Had not an ear to hear my true time broke.

49.play: I wasted time, and now doth time waste me;

50.play: For now hath time made me his numbering clock:

51.play: My thoughts are minutes; and with sighs they jar

52.play: Their watches on unto mine eyes, the outward watch,

53.play: Whereto my finger, like a dial's point,

54.play: Is pointing still, in cleansing them from tears.

55.play: Now sir, the sound that tells what hour it is

56.play: Are clamorous groans, which strike upon my heart,

57.play: Which is the bell: so sighs and tears and groans

58.play: Show minutes, times, and hours: but my time

59.play: Runs posting on in Bolingbroke's proud joy,

60.play: While I stand fooling here, his Jack o' the clock.

61.play: This music mads me; let it sound no more;

62.play: For though it have holp madmen to their wits,

63.play: In me it seems it will make wise men mad.

64.play: Yet blessing on his heart that gives it me!

65.play: For 'tis a sign of love; and love to Richard

66.play: Is a strange brooch in this all-hating world.
